focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

An old Dey aunt with one cloudy eye and a yellow oil apron shoved a bucket into Daro's hands before Iven had the mooring rope properly around the post. People came down the steps in rolled trousers and aprons and rain capes, not pilgrims or gawkers but Lantern Quay folk with the brisk impatience of people who had already decided the matter and did not see why strangers should slow it down.

Riva still had the ferry pole braced against the slick boards, holding the hull steady with the stiff mean care she used when exhaustion was one bad decision away from turning into temper. Daro sat near the bow with his shoulders fallen in, beautiful and used up. Lale had Nami's bad hand in her lap, changing the cloth around the cut with those indigo-stained nails that always made her look cleaner than anybody else even when she had gone two days without proper sleep. Nami kept trying to look over her own bandage as if curiosity might lower the fever.

Fish skin blistered somewhere over charcoal. Rice went sweet under the lid. Lamp oil cut with orange peel made the whole wet village smell like somebody stubbornly insisting on comfort in weather that did not deserve it. Stilt houses stood on their long black legs, and smoke slipped under the eaves instead of up. Prayer bowls hung on nails to dry, copper and clay and lacquered gourd, their rims clicking together in the wind like teeth. Children had already stolen half a dozen and were racing them through a runnel beside the steps, crouched in the mud and shrieking every time one tipped and filled. Iven had been wet so long his shirt felt grown into him. The first breath of cooking hit his empty stomach hard enough that he had to sit down on the gunwale and pretend he only meant to retie the mooring rope.

"Hold that if you...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...arm back if the hook line blurs. And don't let anybody grab you by the wrist when the current turns."

Nami touched the river mint behind her ear to make sure it was still there. "Will the marks really tell her when to turn?"

"If she pays attention." Paska bent to pick the ledger up from beside the cat and held it out to Lale. "Skin's honest longer than paper. That's the lesson. Try not to waste it."

Lale took the book with her unmarked hand. The gap in the binding felt uglier now that it had a cause she could not blame on anybody else. She tucked the ledger under her arm, careful of the fresh bandage, and followed the others out onto the walkway.

The market had resumed itself below them because markets always did. A man was gutting silver fish under a patched awning. A girl with scarlet glass bangles was haggling over lamp oil because flood and corpse and saint family still didn't change the price of ordinary life. Rain began again in a fine slant, not enough to drive anyone indoors. The water under the pilings looked blacker than ink.

Riva went ahead toward the mooring lane and the Bitter Thread. Daro lingered half a step behind Lale, thinking. Iven dropped to the rear. Nami moved wherever there was room, all alertness and wet hair and dangerous openness. The family kept its old shape even now, each person falling where habit and injury placed them.

Lale felt the wrapped marks pull when she closed her hand around the rail. The sting was immediate and exact. That was good. Let the body keep the account for once. Let it refuse neatness. Let the phrase sit where she could not lock it in a drawer.

The words sixth mouth had been ink once, then paper, then absence. Now they moved warm under her own skin as she stepped toward Inkwater and the waiting turn of the tide.
